chunk_summary: This section discusses screening metric variations for moderate acute malnutrition (MAM) in India, emphasizing the use of MUAC and WHZ/WLZ, documentation strategies, and risk-based care to address discrepancies in clinical and programmatic criteria.
learning_objectives: Understand the differences between MUAC and WHZ/WLZ as screening tools for MAM in India.
Learn the documentation strategy for managing discrepancies in MAM screening metrics.
Recognize the importance of risk-based care and clinical stability in MAM treatment decisions.
key_facts: MUAC and WHZ/WLZ are both recommended by WHO for identifying moderate wasting.
The Ministry of Women and Child Development (MoWCD) in India reserves MUAC for emergencies like pandemics or natural disasters.
Discrepancies between MUAC and WHZ/WLZ can result in children being excluded from local entitlement pathways.
Clinical strategy recommends recording both MUAC and WHZ/WLZ, using the more severe measure for risk stratification.
Caregivers should receive written documentation of both measures to ensure continuity of care.
reference_quote: Medical definitions and the WHO guideline include both MUAC and WHZ/WLZ for identifying moderate wasting.
document_key_topics: Screening metric variations for MAM in India
Risk-based care and clinical decision-making in MAM
